text.title: Writing Implementation Plans Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every writing-implementation-plans application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: The plan MUST NOT be authored until design.md frontmatter shows `status: Accepted`. Plans built on Draft designs re-shard mid-wave when an AD-X changes. The Accepted-design gate is non-negotiable.
rule.rationale: Re-sharding mid-execution is the most expensive recovery operation in SDD. The Accepted-design gate prevents it.

rule.statement: Every task in the plan MUST trace to ≥1 file_table row from design.md. New files appearing in the plan but not in design's file_table are forbidden — they indicate scope drift past the design gate.
rule.rationale: Untraced files become forgotten dependencies. The file_table is the contract; the plan honors it.

rule.statement: Each task in a wave MUST have an `est_tokens` budget under 100000. Tasks exceeding this MUST be split before the wave is scheduled. The aggregate of parallel tasks in one wave SHOULD NOT exceed 5x single-task budget on a single executor stream.

rationale.source: Claude Code execution telemetry, wave-orchestrator data

rule.rationale: Tasks over 100k tokens hit context limits mid-execution; aggregate-overrun waves swamp the executor pool. Pre-split + budget-cap protects throughput.

rule.statement: Inter-task dependencies MUST be modelled as Finish-to-Start (FS) edges and listed explicitly per task. Implicit deps via shared-file modifications MUST be surfaced (run `find-shared-files.py` from `workflow-design-phase` scripts) and either de-duplicated or modelled as explicit FS.
rule.rationale: Implicit shared-file deps are the leading cause of failed waves: two tasks land on the same file, second blows up on merge. Explicit FS edges + dedup are the cheap fix.

rule.statement: The plan MUST include a Rollout section naming feature-flag, canary, or staged-release strategy AND a Rollback section naming the reversal procedure for the last wave. Plans missing rollback are forbidden — irreversible by design.
rule.rationale: Plans without a rollback path produce incidents that cannot be resolved without forward-only fixes. The Rollback section is the cheap insurance.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
